---
title: "Building a Modern, Reproducible Data Science Workflow"
author: "Will Doyle"
date: "2026-03-31"
output: github_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  eval    = FALSE   # most chunks are illustrative; set eval=TRUE selectively
)
```

---

# Overview

This lecture steps back from modeling to ask a question that matters as much
as model accuracy: *how do you organize a data science project so that it is
reproducible, collaborative, and maintainable?*

We have spent this course building sophisticated models. But a model that
cannot be reproduced, that lives inside a single monolithic script, or that
breaks when a package updates, is not a finished product — it is a liability.

By the end of this lecture you will be able to:

- Initialize a project correctly from a GitHub repository
- Establish and document project conventions *before* writing analysis code
- Structure a research compendium that separates concerns across files
- Use `{targets}` to build a dependency-aware analysis pipeline
- Apply `renv` and `here` to achieve environment and path reproducibility
- Write a paper-style Rmd that contains *no computation*, only results

---

# 1. Start with GitHub, Not with Code

The single most common workflow mistake is writing code first and thinking
about organization later. By the time you realize your project needs
structure, you have already made dozens of implicit decisions that are hard
to undo.

**The correct sequence is:**

```
Create GitHub repo → Clone locally → Set up infrastructure → Write code
```

Never the reverse.

## 1.1 Create the Repository on GitHub First

Go to GitHub and create a new repository *before opening RStudio*. This
matters for several reasons:

- The repo is the unit of the project. Everything lives inside it.
- It forces you to name the project before you start — a useful discipline.
- Cloning gives you a clean `.git/` history from the first commit.
- Collaborators (or your future self) can clone and immediately have a
  working copy.

**Naming conventions for repos:**

```
# Good: lowercase, hyphens or underscores, descriptive
predict_major
els-college-outcomes
nnet-wage-prediction

# Bad: generic, dated, ambiguous
analysis_final
project2_v3
NEW_FINAL_USE_THIS
```

The name you give the repo *is* the name you give the project. Choose it
carefully and do not change it.

## 1.2 Clone, Don't Create

After creating the repo on GitHub, clone it to your machine:

```bash
# In the terminal (not the R console)
git clone https://github.com/yourusername/predict_major.git
cd predict_major
```

Then open the cloned folder as an RStudio project (`File → Open Project` or
`File → New Project → Existing Directory`). RStudio will detect the `.git/`
folder and activate git integration automatically.

**Never use `File → New Project → New Directory` for a project that will
live on GitHub.** That workflow creates the project locally first and
requires manual git initialization — an extra step that students frequently
skip or do incorrectly.

---

# 2. Conventions Before Code

The second most common workflow mistake is jumping into writing functions
before agreeing on how files will be named, how scripts will communicate
with each other, and what the output format will be.

Conventions are rules the project follows consistently. They reduce
cognitive overhead because you never have to remember *how* something is
done — you just apply the rule. A small set of well-chosen conventions is
worth more than a large set of loosely followed ones.

## 2.1 The Conventions We Follow in This Course

We will specify these conventions at the start of every project and record
them in the `README.md`. Here are the ones I use in `predict_major`:

**File naming**

```
# All files: snake_case, no spaces, no dates
wrangle.R       # good
Wrangle Final.R # bad — spaces and capital letter
wrangle_v2.R    # bad — version number in filename (git handles versions)
```

**Pipes**

```r
# Always %>% (magrittr), never base R |>
# Reason: consistent with tidyverse; |> has subtle behavioral differences
# with some tidyverse functions (e.g., argument placeholders)
data %>% filter(x > 0) %>% select(y)   # correct
data |> filter(x > 0) |> select(y)     # avoid in this project
```

**Paths**

```r
# Always here::here(), never setwd(), never absolute paths, never ../
here::here("data", "raw", "els.dta")   # correct
"/Users/will/projects/predict_major/data/raw/els.dta"  # wrong — breaks on any other machine
"../../data/raw/els.dta"               # wrong — breaks if directory depth changes
```

**How scripts communicate**

```r
# Scripts do not source each other.
# Objects are passed through the targets pipeline.
# A function in R/wrangle.R produces an object.
# _targets.R defines a target that calls that function.
# Other targets read the cached output via tar_read() / tar_load().
```

**No side effects in R/ functions**

```r
# Functions in R/ should:
#   - take inputs as arguments
#   - return a value
#   - never write files (except figure-saving functions, which return the path)
#   - never call library() or require()
#   - never set options() or par()

# Good
make_analytic_sample <- function(els_clean) {
  els_clean %>% filter(f2evratt == 1, !is.na(cip2))
}

# Bad
make_analytic_sample <- function() {
  library(tidyverse)            # bad: side effect
  load("data/els_clean.rda")   # bad: reads from fixed path, not an argument
  els_clean <<- els_clean %>%  # bad: global assignment
    filter(f2evratt == 1)
}
```

**Iteration style**

```r
# Always purrr (map/map2/walk/etc.) over for loops for functional tasks
# Reason: explicit about input/output types, composable, no loop variable leakage

# Good
results <- map(models, ~ extract_metrics(.x))

# Avoid
results <- list()
for (i in seq_along(models)) {
  results[[i]] <- extract_metrics(models[[i]])
}
```

**Random seeds**

```r
# Set once per target that involves randomness.
# Use a project-wide constant — we use 2002 (ELS base year).
# Document it in _targets.R so the choice is visible and intentional.
set.seed(2002)
```

## 2.2 Where to Document Conventions

Record conventions in two places:

1. **`README.md`** — a "Contributing" or "Code conventions" section that
   anyone cloning the repo reads first.

2. **Comments in `_targets.R`** — because `_targets.R` is the entry point
   for anyone trying to understand the pipeline, it is a natural place to
   state the rules that govern how the pipeline is organized.

---

# 3. Project Structure: Separation of Concerns

A well-organized project has *one place for each kind of thing*. This
principle is called **separation of concerns**. Violation looks like this:

```r
# analysis.R — everything in one file (don't do this)
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
els <- read_dta("els.dta")
els <- els %>% filter(f2evratt == 1)
rec <- recipe(cip2 ~ ., data = els) %>% step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())
fit <- workflow() %>% add_recipe(rec) %>% add_model(mlp() %>% set_engine("keras")) %>% fit(els)
predict(fit, els)
# ... 800 more lines
```

This is common and it is fragile. Changing the recipe requires you to hunt
through the file for where the recipe ends and the model begins. Rerunning
the model means rerunning the data wrangling. Extracting a figure for a
presentation means copying code out of context.

## 3.1 The Structure You _Should_ Use

```
predict_major/
│
├── _targets.R          # Pipeline definition — the spine of the project
│
├── R/                  # Function definitions only — no side effects
│   ├── wrangle.R       # Data loading, recoding, analytic sample
│   ├── recipe.R        # Preprocessing recipe construction
│   ├── model.R         # Model spec and workflow
│   ├── fit.R           # Tuning, parameter selection, final fit
│   ├── evaluate.R      # Metrics, predictions, confusion matrix
│   ├── importance.R    # Variable importance
│   ├── visualize.R     # All ggplot figures
│   └── tables.R        # All tables
│
├── paper/
│   ├── paper.Rmd       # Manuscript — no computation, only tar_read()
│   ├── appendix.Rmd    # Online appendix
│   └── references.bib  # BibTeX bibliography
│
├── data/
│   ├── raw/            # Original data (gitignored if restricted-use)
│   ├── processed/      # Analytic datasets (gitignored, regenerated by pipeline)
│   └── codebook/       # Variable documentation, crosswalks
│
└── outputs/
    ├── figures/        # Saved plots (.pdf + .png)
    ├── tables/         # Saved tables (.rds + .csv)
    └── models/         # Saved model objects (.rds)
```

**The key idea**: `R/` contains definitions; `_targets.R` contains the
plan; `paper/` contains narrative. Nothing crosses those boundaries.

## 3.2 The Thin Paper Principle

The paper Rmd should be *thin*. Its job is to tell a story using results
that already exist. It should contain:

```r
# Good: paper.Rmd only does this
tar_load(performance_metrics)
acc <- performance_metrics %>% filter(.metric == "accuracy") %>% pull(.estimate)
```

```r
# Bad: paper.Rmd should never do this
els <- read_dta(here("data", "raw", "els.dta")) %>%
  filter(f2evratt == 1) %>%
  ...
fit <- workflow() %>% fit(els_train)
```

When computation lives in the paper Rmd:

- Rendering the paper requires rerunning the model (which may take hours)
- Changing a figure requires re-rendering the paper
- A broken model breaks the paper even if the narrative is fine
- There is no audit trail separating "the analysis" from "the write-up"

Keep the paper thin. Let targets do the work.

---

# 4. Reproducibility Infrastructure

Three tools work together to make a project reproducible on any machine,
by any person, at any point in the future.

| Tool | What it solves |
|---|---|
| `git` / GitHub | Tracks *changes to code* over time |
| `renv` | Tracks *which packages* (and which versions) were used |
| `here` | Tracks *where files live* relative to the project root |

You need all three. Each solves a different part of the reproducibility
problem.

## 4.1 `renv`: Locking Package Versions

The problem `renv` solves: you fit a model in November using `tidymodels`
0.2.1. A collaborator clones your repo in February after `tidymodels` 0.3.0
has been released. Behavior has changed. Results differ. Nobody knows why.

`renv` creates a **lockfile** (`renv.lock`) that records the exact version
of every package your project uses. Anyone who clones the repo and runs
`renv::restore()` gets identical package versions.

```r
# One-time setup for a new project
renv::init()         # creates renv/ directory and renv.lock

# After installing or updating any package
renv::snapshot()     # updates renv.lock to reflect current state

# After cloning a repo with an existing renv.lock
renv::restore()      # installs the exact package versions recorded
```

**What to commit to git:**

```
# Commit these
renv.lock           # the version record — always commit
renv/activate.R     # the bootstrap script — always commit
.Rprofile           # activates renv on project open — always commit

# Do NOT commit these (add to .gitignore)
renv/library/       # the installed packages — large, machine-specific
```

**The `.Rprofile` that activates `renv` automatically:**

```r
# .Rprofile
source("renv/activate.R")
```

With this in place, anyone who opens the project in RStudio will have `renv`
activated without any manual steps.

## 4.2 `here`: Path Independence

The problem `here` solves: a script that works on your machine breaks on
a collaborator's machine because you hardcoded an absolute path, or uses
`../` navigation that breaks if the calling context changes.

`here::here()` constructs file paths relative to the **project root** —
the directory containing the `DESCRIPTION` file (or `.here` file, or
`.Rproj` file). It works correctly regardless of where R's working
directory is set, what operating system is being used, or who is running
the code.

```r
# here() finds the project root and builds the path from there
here::here("data", "raw", "els.dta")
# → /Users/will/projects/predict_major/data/raw/els.dta  (on Will's machine)
# → /home/student/predict_major/data/raw/els.dta          (on any other machine)
# → C:/Users/Student/predict_major/data/raw/els.dta       (on Windows)

# The DESCRIPTION file anchors the root
# (Using DESCRIPTION also makes the project an R package, which renv understands)
```

**Anchor `here` explicitly in `.Rprofile`:**

```r
# .Rprofile
source("renv/activate.R")

if (requireNamespace("here", quietly = TRUE)) {
  here::i_am("DESCRIPTION")
}
```

`here::i_am("DESCRIPTION")` tells `here` exactly which file marks the
project root. This is more robust than relying on `.Rproj` detection.

## 4.3 `git`: Version Control for Code

You already know how to use git. A few discipline points that matter for
research projects specifically:

**Commit messages should describe intent, not mechanics. answer the question, "why did you do make changes to this file?**

```bash
# Bad
git commit -m "fixed stuff"
git commit -m "updated wrangle.R"

# Good
git commit -m "add missingness indicators to recipe for transcript GPA vars"
git commit -m "collapse CIP codes 5 and 28 into other category (n < 30)"
git commit -m "switch tuning metric from accuracy to macro-weighted ROC AUC"
```

**Commit logical units of work, not daily dumps:**

```bash
# Bad: one giant commit at end of day
git add -A && git commit -m "day's work"

# Good: one commit per meaningful change
git add R/recipe.R && git commit -m "add step_indicate_na for transcript vars"
git add R/fit.R    && git commit -m "expand tuning grid from 10 to 20 points"
```

**Use `.gitignore` deliberately:**

```
# .gitignore — what NOT to track

# Sensitive / restricted data
data/raw/
data/processed/

# Reproducible artifacts (can be regenerated by pipeline)
_targets/
outputs/models/

# Machine-specific / large
renv/library/
.Rhistory
.DS_Store
```

The `.gitignore` is itself a form of documentation — it communicates which
files are inputs (tracked) and which are outputs (regenerated).

---

# 5. The `{targets}` Pipeline

`{targets}` is an R package for defining **dependency-aware analysis
pipelines**. It is the modern successor to `{drake}` and the R equivalent
of tools like `make` or `snakemake` in other languages.

## 5.1 The Core Idea

A `targets` pipeline is a list of **targets** — named objects that are
produced by functions. Each target declares what it depends on. `targets`
builds a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of dependencies and runs only what
needs to run.

```r
# _targets.R — minimal example
library(targets)
tar_source("R/")    # loads all functions from R/

list(
  tar_target(raw_data,      load_data("data/raw/els.dta")),
  tar_target(clean_data,    wrangle(raw_data)),        # depends on raw_data
  tar_target(model_fit,     fit_model(clean_data)),    # depends on clean_data
  tar_target(performance,   evaluate(model_fit))       # depends on model_fit
)
```

When you run `tar_make()`, targets:

1. Checks which targets are out of date (because their function changed,
   their dependencies changed, or they have never been run)
2. Runs only those targets, in dependency order
3. Caches every result to `_targets/`

Change the recipe and re-run `tar_make()`: only the recipe, model, and
evaluation targets re-run. The data loading and wrangling targets are
already cached and are skipped.

## 5.2 Visualizing the Pipeline

```r
library(targets)
tar_visnetwork()    # opens an interactive DAG in the viewer
```

This is one of `targets`' most valuable features. The DAG makes the
dependency structure of your analysis explicit and visible. It is also
useful for communicating the analysis to collaborators and reviewers.

```r
tar_manifest()      # tabular view of all targets and their commands
tar_outdated()      # which targets need to be rerun
tar_progress()      # status of each target after tar_make()
```

## 5.3 Reading Targets in the Paper

In `paper.Rmd` and `appendix.Rmd`, use `tar_read()` and `tar_load()` to
access cached targets. No computation happens in the paper — these calls
simply retrieve already-computed objects from the `_targets/` cache.

```r
# Load a single object into the environment
tar_load(performance_metrics)

# Read a single object without loading into environment
acc <- tar_read(performance_metrics) %>%
  filter(.metric == "accuracy") %>%
  pull(.estimate)

# Reference inline
```

The result: **every number in the paper is live**. Change the model, rerun
`tar_make()`, re-render the paper — every inline number, table, and figure
updates automatically.

## 5.4 File Targets

Some targets produce files rather than R objects — figures, saved model
weights, CSV exports. These use `format = "file"` and the function must
return the file path:

```r
# In _targets.R
tar_target(
  fig_vip,
  plot_vip(vip_scores),   # function saves the file and returns the path
  format = "file"          # targets tracks the file, not an R object
)

# In R/visualize.R
plot_vip <- function(vip_scores) {
  p <- vip_scores %>% ggplot(aes(...)) + ...
  path <- here::here("outputs", "figures", "fig4_vip.png")
  ggsave(path, p)
  path   # return the path so targets can track it
}
```

With `format = "file"`, targets will re-run the target if the file is
deleted or if the function changes — even if the upstream targets are
unchanged.

## 5.5 Rendering the Paper as a Target

```r
# In _targets.R
tar_render(
  paper,
  here::here("paper", "paper.Rmd"),
  output_dir = here::here("paper")
)
```

`tar_render()` is a `{tarchetypes}` function that treats the Rmd rendering
as a target. It automatically detects which targets are called via
`tar_read()` and `tar_load()` inside the Rmd and adds them as dependencies.
If any of those targets change, the paper target becomes outdated and will
re-render on the next `tar_make()`.

---

# 6. Organizing for Development

A project structure that is correct for a finished product may be clunky to
develop in. Here we discuss how to work with this structure iteratively.

## 6.1 The `fit_model` Flag Pattern

Fitting neural networks is slow. During development you don't want to refit
every time you change a figure. Use a boolean flag at the top of your
targets plan:

```r
# _targets.R
fit_model <- FALSE   # set to TRUE when you want to refit

list(
  ...,
  tar_target(
    tuning_results,
    if (fit_model) {
      tune_model(els_workflow, els_folds, tuning_grid)
    } else {
      tar_read(tuning_results)   # load from cache without refitting
    }
  ),
  ...
)
```

This is the same pattern we use in lecture Rmds. It lets you iterate on
downstream targets (figures, tables, the paper) without paying the cost of
refitting.

## 6.2 Interactive Development vs. Pipeline Development

During development, you often want to run code interactively — not via
`tar_make()`. The two modes coexist cleanly if your functions have no side
effects:

```r
# Interactive development session
tar_load(els_train)          # load the cached training data
tar_load(els_recipe)         # load the cached recipe

# Now you can call functions interactively
rec_prepped <- prep(els_recipe, training = els_train)
baked       <- bake(rec_prepped, new_data = NULL)
glimpse(baked)               # inspect without running the full pipeline
```

The discipline: once your interactive exploration leads to a working
function, move it into `R/` and wire it into `_targets.R`. Never leave
important logic living only in an interactive session.

## 6.3 Branching for Experiments

When you want to try a modeling variant without breaking the main pipeline,
use a separate targets branch rather than commenting out code:

```r
# In _targets.R — compare two model architectures
list(
  ...,
  tar_target(model_spec_base,  build_model_spec(layers = 2)),
  tar_target(model_spec_deep,  build_model_spec(layers = 4)),
  tar_target(fit_base,         fit_final_model(workflow_base, els_split)),
  tar_target(fit_deep,         fit_final_model(workflow_deep, els_split)),
  tar_target(comparison,       compare_fits(fit_base, fit_deep))
)
```

Both fits live in the cache. You can load either interactively. The paper
targets whichever one you wire it to.

## 6.4 What to Commit When

A simple rule: **commit the code, not the cache**.

```bash
# After every meaningful code change
git add R/recipe.R
git commit -m "add missingness indicators to recipe"

# After updating packages
renv::snapshot()
git add renv.lock
git commit -m "snapshot renv after adding kableExtra"

# Never commit
_targets/           # cache — large, machine-specific, regenerated by pipeline
outputs/models/     # same reasoning
data/raw/           # restricted-use data
```

The `renv.lock` and `R/` files together are sufficient for a collaborator
to reproduce every result from scratch.

---

# 7. Putting It Together: The Full Workflow

Here is the complete sequence for starting a new project from scratch,
incorporating everything from this lecture.

## Step 1: Create the GitHub repo

Go to GitHub. New repository. Name it. Add a `README`. Do not add code.

## Step 2: Clone and open

```bash
git clone https://github.com/yourusername/predict_major.git
cd predict_major
# Open in RStudio: File → Open Project → browse to folder
```

## Step 3: Initialize infrastructure

```r
# In R console
install.packages(c("renv", "here", "targets", "tarchetypes"))

# Initialize renv
renv::init()

# Confirm here anchoring (after creating DESCRIPTION)
here::i_am("DESCRIPTION")
```

## Step 4: Create the directory structure

```r
# Create all directories
dirs <- c(
  "R",
  "paper",
  "data/raw", "data/processed", "data/codebook",
  "outputs/figures", "outputs/tables", "outputs/models"
)
purrr::walk(dirs, ~ dir.create(here::here(.x), recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE))
```

## Step 5: Write and commit the conventions

Write the `README.md` with a conventions section before writing any analysis
code. Commit it:

```bash
git add README.md DESCRIPTION .gitignore .Rprofile
git commit -m "initialize project infrastructure and conventions"
```

## Step 6: Stub `_targets.R`

Write `_targets.R` with all target names and their dependencies defined, but
with stub functions that return `NULL`. This defines the shape of the pipeline
before any function bodies exist:

```r
# Stub approach: define the DAG first
list(
  tar_target(els_raw,        load_els(raw_data_path)),       # not written yet
  tar_target(analytic_sample, make_analytic_sample(els_raw)), # not written yet
  ...
)
```

Commit the stub pipeline:

```bash
git add _targets.R
git commit -m "stub pipeline DAG — all target names and dependencies defined"
```

## Step 7: Fill in functions, one file at a time

Work through `R/` files in pipeline order: `wrangle.R` → `recipe.R` →
`model.R` → `fit.R` → `evaluate.R` → `importance.R` → `visualize.R` →
`tables.R`. After each file, run `tar_make()` to check that the pipeline
runs to that point. Commit:

```bash
git add R/wrangle.R
git commit -m "implement load_els, wrangle_els, make_analytic_sample"
```

## Step 8: Write the paper last

Only after `tar_make()` runs cleanly to the `tables` and `figures` targets
should you open `paper.Rmd`. By then, every `tar_read()` call has a real
object behind it and the paper renders on the first attempt.

---

# 8. Face Validity Checks Throughout

At each stage of the pipeline, build in a face validity check before moving
on. These are quick, informal checks that something is roughly right — not
formal tests, but also not blind faith.

```r
# After wrangling
analytic_sample %>% count(cip2) %>% print(n = Inf)        # expected: ~15-25 categories
analytic_sample %>% summarise(n = n())                     # expected: several thousand
analytic_sample %>% summarise(across(everything(), ~ mean(is.na(.x)))) # spot missing

# After recipe
recipe_check$n_predictors   # expected: substantially more than raw vars (due to one-hot)
recipe_check$n_rows         # expected: same as nrow(els_train)

# After tuning
collect_metrics(tuning_results) %>%
  filter(.metric == "roc_auc") %>%
  arrange(desc(mean))      # expected: best > chance (1/n_classes)

# After final fit
performance_metrics        # does accuracy exceed the null classifier?
```

The null classifier — always predicting the most common class — sets the
floor. A model that does not clearly beat the null classifier is not useful,
regardless of how sophisticated the architecture is.

---

# Summary

| Principle | Implementation |
|---|---|
| Start with GitHub | Create repo first; clone before writing code |
| Document conventions | `README.md` conventions section before analysis |
| Separate concerns | `R/` for functions, `_targets.R` for plan, `paper/` for narrative |
| Reproducible environments | `renv` for packages, `here` for paths |
| Dependency-aware pipelines | `{targets}` tracks what needs to rerun |
| Thin papers | `paper.Rmd` uses only `tar_read()`, never computes |
| Commit strategically | Code and `renv.lock` yes; cache and data no |
| Check face validity | At every pipeline stage, before moving on |

---

# Further Reading

- Marwick, B., Boettiger, C., & Mullen, L. (2018). Packaging data analytical
  work reproducibly using R (and friends). *The American Statistician*, 72(1),
  80–88. https://doi.org/10.1080/00031305.2017.1375986

- Landau, W. M. (2021). The targets R package: A dynamic Make-like
  function-oriented pipeline toolkit for reproducibility and
  high-performance computing. *Journal of Open Source Software*, 6(57),
  2959. https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.02959

- Bryan, J. (2017). *Project-oriented workflow*.
  https://www.tidyverse.org/blog/2017/12/workflow-vs-script/

- Kuhn, M., & Silge, J. (2022). *Tidy modeling with R*.
  https://www.tmwr.org

- The targets manual: https://books.ropensci.org/targets/
